audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Jerusalem entry, colt, Hosanna, fig tree frame, temple action, house of prayer/cave of robbers, trust/prayer, forgiveness, and authority dispute.

verses.restored: When they draw near to Jerusalem, to Bethphage and Bethany, toward the Mount of Olives, he sends two of his disciples
and says to them, Go into the village opposite you, and immediately entering it you will find a colt tied, on which no human has yet sat. Loose it and bring it.
If anyone says to you, Why are you doing this? say, The Lord has need of it, and immediately he sends it back here.
They went and found a colt tied at a door outside in the street, and they loose it.
Some standing there said to them, What are you doing, loosing the colt?
They said to them just as Jesus had said, and they allowed them to go.
They bring the colt to Jesus, throw their garments on it, and he sat on it.
Many spread their garments on the way, and others spread leafy branches cut from the fields.
Those going before and those following cried out, Hosanna! Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord!
Blessed is the coming reign of our father David! Hosanna in the highest!
He entered Jerusalem and the temple. Looking around at everything, since the hour was already late, he went out to Bethany with the twelve.
On the next day, when they came out from Bethany, he was hungry.
Seeing from afar a fig tree having leaves, he went to see if perhaps he would find anything on it. Coming to it, he found nothing except leaves, for it was not the season of figs.
He answered and said to it, May no one eat fruit from you any longer into the age. His disciples were hearing.
They came to Jerusalem. Entering the temple, he began to cast out those selling and those buying in the temple, and overturned the tables of the money-changers and the seats of those selling doves.
He would not allow anyone to carry a vessel through the temple.
He was teaching and saying to them, Has it not been written: My house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations? But you have made it a cave of robbers.
The chief priests and scribes heard and were seeking how they might destroy him, for they feared him, because the whole crowd was struck with wonder at his teaching.
When evening came, they went out of the city.
Passing by in the morning, they saw the fig tree withered from the roots.
Peter remembered and said to him, Rabbi, look, the fig tree that you cursed has withered.
Jesus answered them, Have trust of the Divine.
Amen, I say to you: whoever says to this mountain, Be lifted up and thrown into the sea, and does not doubt/divide in the heart but trusts that what is spoken happens, it will be for that one.
Because of this I say to you: all things whatever you pray and ask, trust that you received, and it will be for you.
Whenever you stand praying, release if you have anything against anyone, so that your Father in the heavens also may release you your trespasses.
[Some later manuscripts add: But if you do not release, neither will your Father in the heavens release your trespasses.]
They came again into Jerusalem. As he was walking in the temple, the chief priests, scribes, and elders came to him,
and said to him, By what authority do you do these things? Or who gave you this authority to do these things?
Jesus said to them, I will ask you one word; answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I do these things.
The baptism of John, was it from heaven or from humans? Answer me.
They reasoned among themselves, saying, If we say, From heaven, he will say, Why then did you not trust him?
But shall we say, From humans? They feared the crowd, for all held John to truly be a prophet.
Answering Jesus, they said, We do not know. Jesus said to them, Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things.
